tests_to_run: uv run python -c 'import json; json.load(open("docs/schemas/rig.relay.builder_publication_record.v1.schema.json"))' — validate JSON is parseable
Manual spot-check: validate publication_record.v1.json and v2.json against new schema to ensure backward compatibility
bun typecheck (packages/opencode)

type_errors_risk: No TypeScript type errors — the schema is JSON, not TS.

RISK: publication_record.v1.json (22 fields) and v2.json (14 fields) have DIFFERENT STRUCTURES. v1 has inline `prepublication_review_cycle` and `tests_run[]`; v2 has flat `implemented_capabilities[]`. If the schema matches only one version, the other will fail validation.

RISK: publications.v1.jsonl has a THIRD field set (checkpoint_sha, branch, boundary, changed_files[], deferred_seams[], prepublication_verdict, publisher_session, published_at) — if the schema is based purely on v2, the ledger format won't validate.
Design recommendation: use oneOf/anyOf or separate schema files for the record vs. ledger formats.

type_errors_risk: No TypeScript type errors from schema itself.

HIGH RISK: The three prepublication tools (admitted/blocked/inconclusive) write ad-hoc JSON with NO schema validation. If the new schema introduces structural differences (e.g., nesting round details under a rounds[] array instead of flat review_round field), the tools MUST be updated. This is a production code change, not just a schema addition.

RISK: Tool.writeFileString uses fs.writeFileString — no type safety at the boundary. Schema field names are in string literals, not typed. Any mismatch between schema field names and tool field names will only surface during runtime validation, not at compile time.

RISK: Three tools share similar but not identical field sets (admitted_at vs issued_at, notes vs reason vs blocker_description). Schema must use optional fields or discriminatedUnion to handle all three variants.

type_errors_risk: HIGH RISK: Snapshot.FileDiff has optional fields for backward compatibility — if the comparison engine assumes all fields are present, it will get undefined errors.
MEDIUM RISK: Campaign/binder.ts Binder persistence is STUBBED — if the review engine tries BinderService.getBinders(), it will get [] instead of real data. Must not block on binder data.
HIGH RISK: Plan claim_atoms are free-text strings (no file path structure). The comparison engine's fuzzy matching tier (GAP-002) must handle parsing file mentions from prose, which is brittle.
MEDIUM RISK: review/ may depend on schemas (items 1-4) that don't exist yet — design forward-looking but implement after schema creation.

HIGH RISK: Effect.fnUntraced vs Effect.fn style conventions — the codebase uses specific patterns (see packages/opencode/AGENTS.md). Incorrect Effect composition patterns will cause type errors or runtime panics.

bun test (packages/opencode) — full suite, especially tool registry tests

type_errors_risk: CRITICAL: If import path in registry.ts is wrong (./review-manifest vs ./review-manifest.ts), the module won't resolve at all — build failure.
MEDIUM: If Tool.define() returns wrong type or parameters Schema doesn't match expected input, the tool will register but fail validation at runtime.
HIGH: Effect composition errors in the tool's execute function — common failure mode for Effect-based tools.
HIGH: If the tool depends on review/ engine (item 6) and that engine doesn't exist yet, the import will fail at compile time. The tool AND engine must be implemented together.
LOW: If role-contracts.ts misses the new tool name in an allowlist, certain agents won't be able to call it (which might be intentional — only historian should have access?).

critical_path: item-2 (builder_publication_record schema) → item-4 (prepublication_review_cycle schema) → item-1 (review_manifest schema) → item-5 (IN-002 plan) → item-6 (review/ engine) → item-7 (review-manifest tool)
critical_gates: item-2 in-flight: v1 vs v2 publication_record format reconciliation
item-4 tool alignment: prepublication-admitted.ts/blocked.ts/inconclusive.ts have ad-hoc field sets that must match new schema
item-6 risk: binder.ts persistence is STUBBED — review engine must not depend on binder data or must handle empty results gracefully
item-6 risk: claim_atoms are free-text strings (GAP-002) — fuzzy matching is inherently fragile
item-7 blocking: tool is blocked by ALL previous items — it's the terminal dependency

recommended_execution_order: Phase 1a: Create item-2 (builder_publication_record schema) — reconcile v1 (22 fields) and v2 (14 fields) formats
Phase 1b: Create item-3 (verification_record schema) — no existing artifacts to reconcile, design forward-looking
Phase 1c: Create item-4 (prepublication_review_cycle schema) — align with three prepublication tool output formats
Phase 1d: Create item-1 (review_manifest schema) — aggregate contract referencing items 2-4
Phase 2a: Establish item-5 (IN-002 plan) — finalize claim_atoms for the review compression system itself
Phase 2b: Build item-6 (review/ engine) — comparison engine + weight rubric using Snapshot.Service
Phase 3: Build item-7 (review-manifest tool) — register in registry.ts, add to role contracts
Phase 4: Fix GAP-002 (claim_atoms structured file references) — after first working review manifest, upgrade plan artifacts to include `files` field

IN-003 (Evidence-Gated Push): SHARED SCHEMAS: items 2, 3, 4 are also missing for IN-003 — IN-003 cartography identified same gap (GAP-001). These three schemas serve BOTH lanes.
SEAM: If IN-003 creates these schemas first with different field sets, IN-002 must consume them as-is. Coordination needed.
SEAM: IN-003 plans a push-gate that reads prepublication_review_cycle data — same schema dependency.
propose-plan tool (existing): SEAM: claim_atoms format (free-text strings) is a known gap. IN-002's comparison engine must work around it. A future GAP-002 fix should upgrade plan artifacts to include `files` field — this will require revising the propose-plan tool's Parameters schema.
SEAM: ~90+ existing plan files all use free-text claim_atoms. Any format change must be backward-compatible.
